steps.title: Frame the blank
steps.content: The sentence says a linguist “could ______ that assumption with data from a corpus” — and the colon that follows introduces the data: “great” is the sixth most commonly used adjective. The corpus data is being used as evidence to support (not contradict, not rearrange, not logically entail) the assumption. The blank calls for a word meaning “back up with evidence.”

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
Test each option:
  • Choice A — ‘entail’: means “logically require or necessitate.” Empirical corpus data can support an assumption but cannot logically entail it; entailment is a relation between statements, not between data and a hypothesis. Wrong word class for the context. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘transpose’: means “move or swap the order of things.” The linguist is not rearranging the assumption; the word is entirely off-topic. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘refute’: means “prove wrong.” The corpus data confirms the assumption (“great” really does appear often), so the relationship is supportive, not contradictory. Opposite meaning. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘substantiate’: means “provide evidence that backs up a claim.” The ranking of “great” as the sixth most common adjective directly backs up the assumption that it appears quite often. Precise fit. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
